mnemonics.title: SDLC Phases
mnemonics.content: PDEPET (Planning, Design, Encoding, Production/Testing, Evolution/Enancement, Testing)

mnemonics.title: Software Quality Attributes
mnemonics.content: CRISP (Correctness, Reliability, Integrity, Security, Performance, Usability)

mnemonics.title: Agile Values
mnemonics.content: IWF (Individuals over processes, Working software over documentation, Customer collaboration over contracts)

acronyms.usage: Framework for software development phases from requirements to maintenance
Process of ensuring software meets quality standards
Standard language for visualizing and documenting software designs
Fast development methodology using prototypes and iterative development
Algorithm for estimating software development effort and cost

visualTips.visualization: Draw as a staircase descending from top-left to bottom-right, with each step representing a phase. Arrows show downward flow only.
Imagine a spiral starting from center and expanding outward. Each loop represents iteration with risk analysis at the start of each cycle.
Draw a V shape. Left side shows development phases (descending), right side shows corresponding testing phases (ascending).
Picture a circular track with time boxes. Each sprint is a lap where work is planned, executed, and reviewed.
Use box diagrams. Low coupling = boxes with few arrows between them. High cohesion = boxes with all elements tightly packed together.

analogies.analogy: Building a house - first you plan (requirements), draw blueprints (design), lay foundation (implementation), inspect at each stage (testing), move in (deployment), and renovate over time (maintenance).
Going down a waterfall - you start at the top and can only move forward. Once you pass a point, you cannot go back without starting over.
Building with LEGO blocks - you create small pieces iteratively, test they fit, and keep adding pieces until the complete picture emerges.
Walking through a corn maze - you go a short distance, evaluate your position (risk analysis), adjust direction, and continue iteratively until you reach the center.
A sports team - each player has a specific role but works together toward winning. A disorganized team where everyone does everything has low cohesion.
Modular furniture - each piece (module) can be moved or replaced independently without affecting other pieces.
Quality control in manufacturing - each product is inspected at various stages to catch defects before reaching the customer.
